claims.claim: Auth objects must expose both sync_auth_flow and async_auth_flow adapting a shared generator-based auth_flow.
claims.rationale: Auth is shared config on BaseClient but exercised by both sync and async I/O paths.
claims.excerpt: `Auth` exposes both `sync_auth_flow` and `async_auth_flow` methods ... that adapt a shared generator-based `auth_flow`

claims.claim: All shared configuration (auth, headers, cookies, timeouts, redirect policy, event hooks, base URL, trust-env, encoding, lifecycle state) is held on BaseClient.
claims.rationale: Eliminates duplication of configuration surface while keeping I/O implementations separate.
claims.excerpt: Both inherit from a single `BaseClient` (`httpx/_client.py:188`) that holds all I/O-free configuration and state

claims.claim: BaseClient must contain zero I/O methods and only hold I/O-free configuration and state.
claims.rationale: Hoisting logic into BaseClient is only viable for I/O-free logic; mixing sync/async I/O there breaks the parallel design.
claims.excerpt: `BaseClient` contains zero I/O methods.

claims.claim: Client must not await internally and AsyncClient must not block internally; such shortcuts are bugs, not optimizations.
claims.rationale: Each class must be purely sync or purely async; mixing breaks the design.
claims.excerpt: shortcuts that `await` inside `Client` or block inside `AsyncClient` are bugs, not optimizations

claims.claim: Docstring parity between Client and AsyncClient is a maintenance requirement, not a style preference.
claims.rationale: Identical API surface across both classes is load-bearing for documentation and user affordance.
claims.excerpt: Docstring parity between the two classes is a maintenance requirement, not a style preference.

claims.claim: Event hooks may be sync functions (for Client) or coroutine functions (for AsyncClient); BaseClient does not enforce coroutine-ness.
claims.rationale: Hook type depends on which subclass dispatches them; mismatched types produce runtime errors.
claims.excerpt: `BaseClient` doesn't enforce coroutine-ness because the type of hooks depends on which subclass dispatches them.

claims.claim: Every I/O method (send, get, post, put, delete, patch, head, options, stream, request) is implemented twice — once on Client (sync) and once on AsyncClient (async) — with parallel signatures and identical semantics.
claims.rationale: Type safety and clear sync vs async separation require duplicated I/O methods.
claims.excerpt: Every method that hits the wire ... exists twice — once on `Client` (sync) and once on `AsyncClient` (async)

claims.claim: Parallel sync/async class trees match Python ecosystem convention (sqlite3/aiosqlite, psycopg2/asyncpg, redis.Redis/redis.asyncio.Redis).
claims.rationale: Deviating from ecosystem convention would surprise users.
claims.excerpt: Matches Python ecosystem convention. `sqlite3` vs `aiosqlite`, `psycopg2` vs `asyncpg`, `redis.Redis` vs `redis.asyncio.Redis`

claims.claim: Resource management is per-subclass: Client uses __enter__/__exit__ and AsyncClient uses __aenter__/__aexit__; wrong context manager shape raises at entry.
claims.rationale: Prevents accidental misuse of sync/async context management.
claims.excerpt: Users cannot use `with async_client:` or `async with client:` — the wrong context manager shape raises at entry. This is a feature

claims.claim: Runtime polymorphism (single client with runtime dispatch) was rejected because it destroys static type inference and leaks runtime complexity.
claims.rationale: Return types couldn't be statically known and awaitability would depend on runtime inspection.
claims.excerpt: Rejected: destroys type inference (return type can't be statically known), leaks runtime complexity into every call site

claims.claim: Sync vs async choice is made at instantiation time, not at call time; a given instance is never mixed-mode.
claims.rationale: Keeps return types statically resolvable and avoids runtime dispatch complexity.
claims.excerpt: The client choice is made at **instantiation time**, not at call time.

claims.claim: Sync-only with async adapter was rejected because blocking I/O in a thread pool is not true async and locks out trio-native code paths.
claims.rationale: True async support and trio compatibility are requirements.
claims.excerpt: Rejected: defeats the purpose — blocking I/O in a thread pool is not true async, and it locks out `trio`-native code paths.

claims.claim: Tests must cover both sync and async paths; the test suite mirrors the parallel structure with paired sync/async test modules.
claims.rationale: A fix verified only against Client can silently diverge on AsyncClient.
claims.excerpt: Tests must cover both paths.

claims.claim: USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT sentinel distinguishes 'use client-configured default' from 'explicitly disable' (None); both clients use it across I/O method signatures.
claims.rationale: Provides unambiguous semantics for per-request overrides since Python lacks a way to omit parameters callee-visibly.
claims.excerpt: A `USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT` sentinel ... distinguishes "use whatever the client was configured with" from "explicitly disable this parameter" (which uses `None`).

claims.claim: Users should omit parameters rather than explicitly passing USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT; None is reserved for explicit disable.
claims.rationale: USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT is an internal implementation detail exposed only out of necessity.
claims.excerpt: Users should omit the parameter entirely rather than passing `USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT` explicitly; `None` is reserved for explicit disable.

claims.claim: httpx provides two parallel concrete client classes (sync Client and async AsyncClient) instead of a single polymorphic client or sync-only with adapter.
claims.rationale: Python lacks native async/sync polymorphism; parallel classes preserve type inference and make the sync/async choice explicit at instantiation.
claims.excerpt: httpx ships two concrete client classes with matched API surfaces

claims.claim: httpcore exceptions must be mapped to httpx exceptions via HTTPCORE_EXC_MAP; adding a new httpcore exception without mapping leaks httpcore.*Error to users
claims.rationale: Exception translation is the single source of truth for user-facing errors
claims.excerpt: The mapping at `_transports/default.py:71-82` is the single source of truth; adding a new httpcore exception without mapping it leaks `httpcore.*Error` to users.

claims.claim: ASGITransport must work under both asyncio and Trio
claims.rationale: ASGI transport extends the runtime contract and tests verify both runtimes
claims.excerpt: `ASGITransport` must work under both asyncio and Trio (`_transports/asgi.py`)

claims.claim: Direct asyncio.* use (create_task, sleep, Lock) is not allowed outside asyncio-only code paths; prefer anyio abstractions in cross-runtime code
claims.rationale: Direct asyncio use in cross-runtime paths is a Trio regression
claims.excerpt: Reviewers must flag direct `asyncio.*` use outside of asyncio-only code paths. Prefer `anyio` abstractions when synchronization is needed in cross-runtime code.

claims.claim: Documentation should acknowledge the layering so users can file connection-level bugs against httpcore
claims.rationale: Hiding the layering misleads users troubleshooting connection issues
claims.excerpt: The troubleshooting docs should acknowledge this rather than pretending httpx is a single library.

claims.claim: Features must be placed on the correct layer: connection/pooling features in httpcore, auth/event-hook features in httpx
claims.rationale: Landing features on the wrong layer creates the tight coupling this ADR prevents
claims.excerpt: Landing features on the wrong layer creates the tight coupling this ADR exists to prevent.

claims.claim: HTTP/2 support is opt-in via the http2 extras (h2>=3,<5)
claims.rationale: Layering allows HTTP/2 installation without restructuring httpx
claims.excerpt: httpcore's HTTP/2 support is opt-in** via the `http2` extras (`pyproject.toml:45-46` — `h2>=3,<5`)

claims.claim: Only the default transport module imports httpcore; no other httpx module imports httpcore directly
claims.rationale: Enforces the layer boundary via the transport layer
claims.excerpt: No other httpx module imports httpcore directly.

claims.claim: Runtime primitives (events, locks) are selected via sniffio detection at the call site where primitives differ
claims.rationale: Minimal-invasiveness approach avoiding leaking runtime complexity into the user API
claims.excerpt: Runtime detection at the points where primitives differ (event creation, locking) is the minimal-invasiveness approach.

claims.claim: WSGITransport is sync-only and may assume no async context
claims.rationale: Defines scope of WSGI transport
claims.excerpt: `WSGITransport` is sync-only and may assume no async context.

claims.claim: anyio is used for broader abstractions like blocking-thread dispatch or cross-runtime sleeping, not as a full abstraction layer in every call path
claims.rationale: Clarifies role of anyio versus sniffio in the codebase
claims.excerpt: httpx declares `anyio` as a dependency but does not use anyio as a full abstraction layer in every call path.

claims.claim: httpcore is pinned as httpcore==1.*
claims.rationale: Protects httpx from accidental breakage while allowing httpcore to patch; major upgrades require coordinated release
claims.excerpt: httpcore (separate package, pinned as `httpcore==1.*`)

claims.claim: httpx adopts a layered architecture: httpcore handles wire protocol/pooling, httpx handles user-facing API and client semantics
claims.rationale: Separation of concerns; allows independent evolution and lets users depend on either layer
claims.excerpt: httpx adopts a **layered architecture** with a clean split of responsibilities

claims.claim: httpx must support both asyncio and Trio runtimes using sniffio detection and dual-import patterns
claims.rationale: Committing to asyncio-only would fragment the encode stack which ships Trio-based deployments
claims.excerpt: **Trio support is a tested commitment.** `pyproject.toml` declares `"Framework :: AsyncIO"` and `"Framework :: Trio"` classifiers.

claims.claim: httpx's transport abstraction exists independently of httpcore to allow ASGI, WSGI, mocks, or custom transports
claims.rationale: ADR-03 covers why the transport abstraction is needed even though default wraps httpcore
claims.excerpt: it lets users plug in ASGI, WSGI, mocks, or custom transports without any httpcore at all.

claims.claim: sniffio is a hard runtime dependency even though Trio itself is not
claims.rationale: sniffio is tiny and exists precisely for async-library detection
claims.excerpt: **`sniffio` is a hard runtime dependency** even though Trio itself is not.

claims.claim: ASGITransport is async-only; testing ASGI apps requires AsyncClient, while WSGITransport is the sync equivalent
claims.rationale: No sync wrapper hides the event loop for ASGI; sync testing of ASGI apps requires asyncio.run-wrapping
claims.excerpt: Testing with `ASGITransport` is async-only. Users testing ASGI apps must use `AsyncClient(transport=ASGITransport(app))`; there is no sync wrapper that hides the event loop.

claims.claim: Client features (redirects, auth flows, cookie persistence, event hooks, timeout coordination) live above the transport layer and must not be reimplemented in transports
claims.rationale: Separation of concerns: transports handle request-to-response, clients handle higher-level orchestration
claims.excerpt: all implemented in `Client` / `AsyncClient` wrapping calls to `transport.handle_request`. This is deliberate: transports should not re-implement these concerns.

claims.claim: Clients accept a transport parameter; when None, a default httpcore-backed transport is instantiated
claims.rationale: Defines the default transport injection behavior
claims.excerpt: When `transport` is `None`, the default httpcore-backed transport is instantiated.

claims.claim: Clients delegate their __enter__/__exit__ to the underlying transports
claims.rationale: Ensures proper resource cleanup propagates through the transport layer
claims.excerpt: Clients delegate their own `__enter__`/`__exit__` to the underlying transports.

claims.claim: Clients support a mounts parameter mapping URL patterns to transports for per-URL routing
claims.rationale: Enables multi-destination testing patterns where some services are mocked and others use real transports
claims.excerpt: Clients also accept a `mounts` parameter — a `dict[str, Transport]` keyed by URL pattern.

claims.claim: Transport is a first-class pluggable abstraction with two parallel abstract base classes (sync and async)
claims.rationale: Enables swappable transports for testing, in-process ASGI/WSGI, custom pooling, and per-URL routing without monkey-patching
claims.excerpt: Transport is a first-class pluggable abstraction, with two parallel abstract base classes mirroring the sync/async split from ADR-01

claims.claim: ASGITransport supports dual-runtime (asyncio and Trio) via sniffio to uphold runtime-agnosticism
claims.rationale: The transport layer must uphold ADR-02's runtime-agnosticism commitment, not only the user-facing API
claims.excerpt: ASGITransport's dual-runtime support (asyncio and Trio via `sniffio` in `_transports/asgi.py:29-52`) is an ADR-02 consequence

claims.claim: Additions to the base transport classes are breaking changes; prefer extending via new transport classes over expanding the base interface
claims.rationale: Every third-party transport would need to implement new methods or fail
claims.excerpt: Additions to the base classes are breaking changes. Prefer extending via new transport classes over expanding the base interface.

claims.claim: Default transports translate httpcore exceptions to httpx exceptions at the transport boundary
claims.rationale: Prevents leaking httpcore implementation details to users
claims.excerpt: `HTTPTransport` / `AsyncHTTPTransport` map httpcore exceptions to httpx exceptions

claims.claim: Dual-mode transports must provide two separate sync and async classes rather than unifying; MockTransport keeps sync and async code paths distinct
claims.rationale: Follows from the non-unified base class decision
claims.excerpt: "Dual-mode" transports are possible only by providing two separate classes (see `MockTransport`, which supplies both sync and async handler callbacks but keeps the sync and async code paths distinct).

claims.claim: Five transports ship with httpx: HTTPTransport, AsyncHTTPTransport, WSGITransport, ASGITransport, MockTransport
claims.rationale: Documents the built-in transport implementations and their purposes
claims.excerpt: Five transports ship in `httpx/_transports/`

claims.claim: The sync and async base transport classes are not unified under a common ABC; they are siblings and mixing is not supported
claims.rationale: Unifying would require runtime type checks in clients, defeating the type-safety motivation of ADR-01
claims.excerpt: The two base classes are not unified under a common ABC. They are siblings. A transport implementation picks one side or the other; mixing is not supported.

claims.claim: Third-party transports must raise httpx exception types (or subclasses), not httpcore errors directly
claims.rationale: Raising httpcore.*Error directly leaks an implementation detail
claims.excerpt: Third-party transports are expected to raise httpx exception types (or subclasses). Raising `httpcore.*Error` directly leaks an implementation detail.

claims.claim: Transports must be context-manageable, supporting with/async with to release connection pool resources
claims.rationale: Connection pools hold sockets and ASGI transports hold app references; explicit close prevents file descriptor leaks
claims.excerpt: Transports are context-manageable: `with transport:` / `async with transport:` properly releases connection pool resources.

claims.claim: handle_request / handle_async_request is the single method third-party transports must implement; it is stable public API
claims.rationale: Breaking changes to this method break every wrapper in the ecosystem
claims.excerpt: `handle_request` / `handle_async_request` is the single method third-party transports must implement. This is stable API

claims.claim: mounts uses URLPattern matching, a URL-matching DSL supporting all://, scheme, host, port, and wildcards
claims.rationale: Defines the grammar for URL routing in mounts
claims.excerpt: `mounts` uses `URLPattern` matching (`_utils.py`'s `URLPattern` class), a compact URL-matching DSL supporting `all://`, scheme, host, port, and wildcards.

claims.claim: Auth flows must be deterministic and finite; retry limits must be encoded in the generator body
claims.rationale: There is no framework-level guard against infinite loops or unexpected exceptions
claims.excerpt: **Auth flows must be deterministic and finite.** Generators that loop forever or raise at unexpected points create denial-of-service and surprising exception scenarios. Schemes with retry limits must encode the limit in the generator body; there is no framework-level guard.

claims.claim: Auth is implemented as a generator that yields requests and receives responses via generator send()
claims.rationale: Provides a uniform protocol that handles single-leg and multi-leg auth schemes without ad-hoc hooks
claims.excerpt: **Auth is a generator.** The `Auth` base class (`httpx/_auth.py:22-110`) defines a single conceptual method

claims.claim: Auth schemes must declare requires_request_body/requires_response_body when they need to inspect bodies before/during the flow
claims.rationale: The wrappers pre-read only what's declared, preserving the default streaming model
claims.excerpt: An auth scheme that needs to inspect the request body before yielding (e.g., digest auth hashing the body) sets `requires_request_body = True`; the sync/async wrappers ensure the body is materialized before the generator starts.

claims.claim: Auth schemes performing their own I/O must override sync_auth_flow/async_auth_flow directly rather than auth_flow
claims.rationale: I/O shape differs per runtime, so it cannot be expressed in the shared generator
claims.excerpt: Authors of auth schemes that do their own I/O (e.g., fetching a token from disk, calling a remote OAuth endpoint) override `sync_auth_flow` / `async_auth_flow` directly because the I/O shape differs per runtime.

claims.claim: Auth subclasses must follow generator semantics; yielding from non-generator helpers silently fails
claims.rationale: Python's generator protocol is subtle and silent failures are possible
claims.excerpt: Calling `yield` from the wrong place (e.g., inside a helper function that's not a generator) silently fails.

claims.claim: Client.send and AsyncClient.send must not know about specific auth schemes; they only drive the generator
claims.rationale: Adding a new auth scheme doesn't require modifying the client
claims.excerpt: **The client doesn't know about auth schemes.** `Client.send` / `AsyncClient.send` just drive whichever generator they're given.

claims.claim: FunctionAuth wraps a Request -> Request callable as the ergonomic entry point for Basic-auth-like schemes
claims.rationale: Sugar for the common case; complex auth falls through to Auth subclassing
claims.excerpt: **`FunctionAuth`'s simple callable shape** (`httpx/_auth.py:113-123`) is the ergonomic entry point for Basic-auth-like schemes that don't need the full generator. It wraps a `Request -> Request` function.

claims.claim: Per-request auth passed to .send() overrides the client default and is scoped to that call with no global state
claims.rationale: Natural per-request auth configuration without mutable global state
claims.excerpt: A request can have its own auth (overriding the client default) just by passing `auth=` to `.send()`. The auth flow is scoped to that call; no global state.

claims.claim: Setting requires_request_body=True buffers the full body in memory, which may be unacceptable for large uploads
claims.rationale: Streaming auth schemes with body-dependent signatures aren't supported by the current model
claims.excerpt: When `requires_request_body = True`, the body is read and buffered in memory before the flow starts. This may be unacceptable for large uploads.

claims.claim: Stateful auth objects (like DigestAuth caching nonce-count) require locking when reused across concurrent requests
claims.rationale: Auth objects are typically stateless, but stateful exceptions need synchronization
claims.excerpt: Digest auth is a notable exception — it caches nonce-count state. Reusing a stateful auth object across concurrent requests requires locking

claims.claim: The Auth generator protocol is stable public API; changes break third-party ecosystem auth libraries
claims.rationale: Libraries like httpx-auth and httpx-oauth depend on this contract
claims.excerpt: Third-party auth libraries (e.g., `httpx-auth`, `httpx-oauth`) depend on this contract. Any change to the generator protocol (e.g., changing what the driver sends back, or when) breaks every ecosystem auth library. Treat as stable public API.

claims.claim: USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT sentinel must distinguish 'use client auth' from auth=None (explicitly no auth)
claims.rationale: Allows per-request auth overrides without ambiguity
claims.excerpt: The `USE_CLIENT_DEFAULT` sentinel distinguishes "use client auth" from `auth=None` (explicitly no auth).

claims.claim: auth_flow must be a plain generator (not a coroutine) so the same implementation works under both sync and async drivers
claims.rationale: Enables one implementation per auth scheme that works in both runtimes
claims.excerpt: Because `Auth.auth_flow` is a plain generator (not a coroutine), the same generator works under both sync and async drivers.

claims.claim: requires_request_body/requires_response_body default to False; falsely claiming no need silently breaks auth
claims.rationale: Conservative default preserves streaming; incorrect override breaks correctness
claims.excerpt: Overriding them falsely (claiming not to need what you need) silently breaks auth; the default of `False` is conservative for streaming.

claims.claim: requires_response_body=True forces the response body to be read, defeating streaming for large responses
claims.rationale: Fine for small challenges (digest), problematic for protocols inspecting large bodies
claims.excerpt: `requires_response_body = True` forces the response body to be read, not streamed.

claims.claim: sync_auth_flow and async_auth_flow wrap the base generator and handle requires_request_body/requires_response_body via read() or aread()
claims.rationale: Separates sync/async I/O concerns from the shared auth protocol logic
claims.excerpt: `Auth.sync_auth_flow` (`httpx/_auth.py:62-85`) and `Auth.async_auth_flow` (`httpx/_auth.py:87-110`) wrap the base generator and handle `requires_request_body` / `requires_response_body` via sync `read()` or async `aread()` respectively.

claims.claim: Auth schemes declaring requires_response_body force body materialization even inside client.stream(...).
claims.rationale: Auth runs before streaming iteration begins, so first-leg response body must be read for schemes like digest.
claims.excerpt: Auth schemes that need response bodies (`requires_response_body` in ADR-04) force materialization.

claims.claim: Accessing response.content on an unread streamed response must raise ResponseNotRead.
claims.rationale: Surfaces lifecycle mistakes as errors rather than returning empty bodies or blocking.
claims.excerpt: Attempting `response.content` on an unread streamed response raises `ResponseNotRead`

claims.claim: Buffer-by-default response behavior is a stable public commitment; switching to stream-by-default would be a major version breaking change.
claims.rationale: Every existing response.content access would raise ResponseNotRead under stream-default.
claims.excerpt: The default-buffer behavior is a stable public commitment; migration to stream-default would be a major version break.

claims.claim: Client.stream(...) is the only supported way to stream; directly constructing a Response for streaming is unsupported.
claims.rationale: Documentation and examples must route users through stream() to ensure lifecycle management.
claims.excerpt: `client.stream(...)` is the only supported way to stream.

claims.claim: Event hooks run at response-ready time before the body is materialized, and may call response.read()/aread() to force materialization.
claims.rationale: Lets hooks that don't need the body skip the cost, while logging-style hooks can opt in.
claims.excerpt: Event hooks run at response-ready time, not at body-materialization time.

claims.claim: Iteration or reads after read()/close() must raise StreamConsumed or StreamClosed.
claims.rationale: Informative errors tell the user exactly which lifecycle mistake was made.
claims.excerpt: Attempting further iteration after `read()` or `close()` raises `StreamConsumed` or `StreamClosed`.

claims.claim: Normal (non-streaming) client calls must fully materialize the response body before returning, populating response.content.
claims.rationale: The common case is small JSON bodies; users should be able to access .content/.text/.json() without ceremony.
claims.excerpt: Normal (non-streaming) calls fully materialize the body before returning.

claims.claim: Request-side streaming uses a generator or file-like object passed as content; it does not require a context manager because its lifecycle is bounded by the single .send() call.
claims.rationale: This ADR governs response streaming; request streaming has different lifecycle constraints.
claims.excerpt: request streaming doesn't require a context manager because its lifecycle is bounded by the single `.send()` call.

claims.claim: Response bodies track read-state as Unread, Read, or Closed.
claims.rationale: Explicit three-state model gives clear lifecycle errors instead of silent empty bodies or blocking.
claims.excerpt: Response bodies track read-state explicitly. The `Response` class (`httpx/_models.py`) distinguishes three states

claims.claim: Slow streaming consumers hold a connection pool slot for the duration of iteration, so clients with small Limits must account for this.
claims.rationale: Slow SSE-style consumers can exhaust small pools if pool sizing is not considered.
claims.excerpt: Clients with small pools (`Limits`) must account for this in their design.

claims.claim: Stream lifecycle is bound to the response via BoundSyncStream/BoundAsyncStream, which record response.elapsed on close and propagate close to the underlying transport stream.
claims.rationale: Ties stream cleanup and timing to the response object; reading after close must raise.
claims.excerpt: Stream lifecycle is bound to response via `BoundSyncStream` / `BoundAsyncStream`

claims.claim: Streaming iteration methods are provided as paired sync/async variants (iter_*/aiter_*).
claims.rationale: Preserves sync/async parity consistent with ADR-01's sync/async split.
claims.excerpt: Streaming iteration methods are paired sync/async

claims.claim: Streaming responses are opt-in via Client.stream/AsyncClient.stream, which must be used as a context manager.
claims.rationale: Exiting the context closes the underlying stream and releases the socket; without the context manager, cleanup is not guaranteed.
claims.excerpt: The `stream()` method is a **context manager** (`@contextmanager` for sync, `@asynccontextmanager` for async). Exiting the context closes the underlying stream

claims.claim: Users choosing streaming under high-throughput conditions should prefer HTTP/2 to avoid blocking other requests on the same connection.
claims.rationale: Under HTTP/1.1 a streaming response blocks other requests on the connection; HTTP/2 multiplexes streams.
claims.excerpt: Users choosing streaming under high-throughput conditions should prefer HTTP/2.

claims.claim: iter_raw/aiter_raw return undecoded bytes with no gzip/brotli/zstd decompression.
claims.rationale: Distinguishes raw transport bytes from decoded content.
claims.excerpt: `response.iter_raw()` / `response.aiter_raw()` — undecoded bytes (no gzip/brotli/zstd decompression).

claims.claim: response.elapsed is only meaningful after the stream is closed, measuring time-to-last-byte for streamed responses.
claims.rationale: BoundSyncStream/BoundAsyncStream record elapsed on close; TTFB must be measured manually.
claims.excerpt: `response.elapsed` is only meaningful after stream close

claims.claim: response.read() is idempotent (returns cached .content); iter_bytes() is not and raises StreamConsumed after prior read/iteration.
claims.rationale: Iteration consumes the stream; reading caches the result — a deliberate distinction.
claims.excerpt: `response.read()` is idempotent but `iter_bytes()` is not.

claims.claim: trust_env toggles whether environment variables are consulted for configuration
claims.rationale: Controls whether external environment influences request behavior
claims.excerpt: Enables or disables usage of environment variables for configuration.

claims.claim: verify parameter accepts True (default CA bundle SSL context), False (disable verification), or an ssl.SSLContext instance for custom context
claims.rationale: Encodes the security default and customization surface for TLS verification
claims.excerpt: Either `True` to use an SSL context with the default CA bundle, `False` to disable verification, or an instance of `ssl.SSLContext` to use a custom context.

claims.claim: DELETE requests should not include a request body, so data, files, json, and content parameters are unavailable
claims.rationale: DELETE semantics typically exclude request bodies
claims.excerpt: the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available on this function, as `DELETE` requests should not include a request body

claims.claim: GET requests should not include a request body, so data, files, json and content parameters are unavailable on this function
claims.rationale: Aligns with HTTP semantics that GET requests should not carry a request body
claims.excerpt: the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available on this function, as `GET` requests should not include a request body

claims.claim: HEAD requests should not include a request body, so data, files, json, and content parameters are not available on this function
claims.rationale: HEAD requests semantically should not carry a body, enforced by omitting body-related parameters
claims.excerpt: the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available on this function, as `HEAD` requests should not include a request body

claims.claim: OPTIONS request function does not accept data, files, json, or content parameters because OPTIONS requests should not include a request body
claims.rationale: OPTIONS requests semantically should not carry a request body, so the API omits body-related parameters
claims.excerpt: the `data`, `files`, `json` and `content` parameters are not available on this function, as `OPTIONS` requests should not include a request body

claims.claim: proxy parameter routes all traffic through a single proxy URL
claims.rationale: Describes routing behavior of the request
claims.excerpt: A proxy URL where all the traffic should be routed.

claims.claim: trust_env controls whether environment variables are used for configuration
claims.rationale: Environment-driven configuration is an architectural toggle affecting behavior across deployments
claims.excerpt: Enables or disables usage of environment variables for configuration.

claims.claim: verify parameter accepts True (default CA bundle SSL context), False (disable verification), or a custom ssl.SSLContext instance
claims.rationale: The parameter encodes a security-relevant default; disabling verification has security implications
claims.excerpt: Either `True` to use an SSL context with the default CA bundle, `False` to disable verification, or an instance of `ssl.SSLContext` to use a custom context.

claims.claim: See Streaming Responses documentation for details
claims.rationale: External reference for further reading
claims.excerpt: See also: [Streaming Responses][0]

claims.claim: Streaming variant of httpx.request that streams the response body instead of loading it fully into memory
claims.rationale: Distinguishes this function from httpx.request by memory/streaming behavior
claims.excerpt: Alternative to `httpx.request()` that streams the response body instead of loading it into memory at once.

claims.claim: Custom authentication schemes are implemented by subclassing Auth and overriding auth_flow().
claims.rationale: Describes the extension mechanism for authentication schemes.
claims.excerpt: To implement a custom authentication scheme, subclass `Auth` and override the `.auth_flow()` method.

claims.claim: Custom authentication schemes are implemented by subclassing Auth and overriding auth_flow().
claims.rationale: Describes the extension mechanism for authentication.
claims.excerpt: To implement a custom authentication scheme, subclass `Auth` and override the `.auth_flow()` method.

claims.claim: When the auth scheme performs I/O or uses synchronization primitives, override sync_auth_flow() and/or async_auth_flow() instead of auth_flow() so Client and AsyncClient use specialized implementations.
claims.rationale: I/O or locking in a generic auth_flow could block or misbehave; specialized sync/async variants are recommended.
claims.excerpt: If the authentication scheme does I/O such as disk access or network calls, or uses synchronization primitives such as locks, you should override `.sync_auth_flow()` and/or `.async_auth_flow()` instead of `.auth_flow()`

claims.claim: Looks up basic auth credentials from a netrc file based on the URL host
claims.rationale: Describes the credential resolution mechanism
claims.excerpt: Use a 'netrc' file to lookup basic auth credentials based on the url host.

claims.claim: If the auth scheme performs I/O or uses synchronization primitives, override sync_auth_flow() and/or async_auth_flow() instead of auth_flow() to provide specialized implementations used by Client and AsyncClient.
claims.rationale: Specialized sync/async implementations are needed when the auth flow blocks or uses locks, so Client and AsyncClient can use the appropriate variant.
claims.excerpt: you should override `.sync_auth_flow()` and/or `.async_auth_flow()` instead of `.auth_flow()` to provide specialized implementations that will be used by `Client` and `AsyncClient` respectively

claims.claim: Default async authentication implementation defers to .auth_flow()
claims.rationale: Documents the default delegation behavior
claims.excerpt: By default, this defers to `.auth_flow()`.

claims.claim: Override the async auth flow method when the authentication scheme performs I/O or uses concurrency primitives

claims.claim: url.query is raw bytes without URL escaping; query strings can only be properly URL escaped when decoding parameter names and values themselves
claims.rationale: Explains why query is exposed as raw bytes
claims.excerpt: A URL query string portion can only be properly URL escaped when decoding the parameter names and values themselves.

claims.claim: url.raw_host is lowercased and IDNA encoded
claims.rationale: Defines representation of raw_host attribute
claims.excerpt: `url.raw_host` is normalized to always be lowercased, and is IDNA encoded.

claims.claim: url.raw_path is raw bytes of path and query without URL escaping, used as the target when constructing HTTP requests; prefer url.path for general use
claims.rationale: raw_path serves a specific protocol role; path is the higher-level interface
claims.excerpt: This portion is used as the target when constructing HTTP requests. Usually you'll want to work with `url.path` instead.

claims.claim: url.scheme is normalized to always be lowercased
claims.rationale: Documents normalization behavior of the scheme attribute
claims.excerpt: `url.scheme` is normalized to always be lowercased.

claims.claim: When a key occurs multiple times in query params, get() returns only the first value
claims.rationale: Documents the selection behavior for duplicate keys
claims.excerpt: If the key occurs more than once, then only the first value is returned.

claims.claim: URL host is always normalized to lowercase with IDNA hosts decoded into unicode
claims.rationale: Consumers can rely on normalized lowercase unicode host representation
claims.excerpt: Always normalized to lowercase, with IDNA hosts decoded into unicode.

claims.claim: multi_items returns all query param items including duplicate keys
claims.rationale: Describes behavior preserving duplicates rather than collapsing them
claims.excerpt: Return all items in the query params. Allow duplicate keys to occur.

claims.claim: Property may be used for generating the value of a request Host header
claims.rationale: Describes intended use
claims.excerpt: This property may be used for generating the value of a request "Host" header.

claims.claim: Value is always normalized to lowercase and IDNA encoded
claims.rationale: Normalization is asserted as invariant
claims.excerpt: Always normalized to lowercase, and IDNA encoded.

claims.claim: URL query parameters are exposed as an immutable multidict
claims.rationale: Immutability and multidict shape define how consumers interact with parsed query parameters
claims.excerpt: neatly parsed and packaged into an immutable multidict representation

claims.claim: The path attribute returns the URL path excluding the query string, URL-decoded
claims.rationale: Describes the semantics of the path property: query string is stripped and percent-encoding is decoded
claims.excerpt: The URL path as a string. Excluding the query string, and URL decoded.

claims.claim: raw_host is always normalized to lowercase and IDNA encoded
claims.rationale: Defines invariant behavior of the raw_host property for consumers relying on its normalized form
claims.excerpt: Always normalized to lowercase, and IDNA encoded.

claims.claim: Target field contains complete URL path and query string as raw bytes, used when constructing HTTP requests
claims.rationale: Describes purpose and format of the target value
claims.excerpt: The complete URL path and query string as raw bytes. Used as the target when constructing HTTP requests.

claims.claim: URL scheme raw bytes are always normalised to lowercase
claims.rationale: Guarantees consistent lowercase representation for scheme comparisons
claims.excerpt: Always normalised to lowercase.

claims.claim: URL scheme is always normalised to lowercase
claims.rationale: Guarantees consistent casing for scheme comparisons
claims.excerpt: Always normalised to lowercase.

claims.claim: URLPattern instances are sortable by priority to enable matching from most specific to least specific
claims.rationale: Sorting by priority supports specificity-ordered matching
claims.excerpt: The priority allows URLPattern instances to be sortable, so that we can match from most specific to least specific.

claims.claim: Length of a file-like stream is determined without reading the stream into memory
claims.rationale: Avoiding loading the stream into memory is an explicit architectural constraint on the implementation
claims.excerpt: return its length in number of bytes without reading it into memory

claims.claim: Boolean values are coerced using JSON-style 'true'/'false' representation
claims.rationale: Establishes a preferred string representation for booleans during coercion
claims.excerpt: we prefer JSON-style 'true'/'false' for boolean values here

claims.claim: Module provides async environment-agnostic concurrency utilities scoped to test usage only
claims.rationale: Indicates the utilities are test-only helpers, not production code
claims.excerpt: Async environment-agnostic concurrency utilities that are only used in tests.

claims.claim: Test fixture isolates os.environ per-test, removing the need to mock os.environ
claims.rationale: Explains the purpose of the utility: keeping environment state clean across tests
claims.excerpt: Keeps os.environ clean for every test without having to mock os.environ
